---
title: "Population-scale discovery of tumor driving gene fusions"
author: "2026 Jacob Krol DBMI Symposium"
format:
  revealjs:
    css: theme.scss
---

## Population-scale discovery of tumor driving gene fusions

<br>

::: {.center}
Jacob Krol
:::

::: {.center}
Layer lab
:::


::: {.center}
Department of Computer Science
:::

::: {.center}
BioFrontiers Institute
:::

![](./images/cu-boulder-logo.png){fig-align="center"}

::: {.center .small}
jacob.krol@colorado.edu
:::

## Fusions


<br>

![](./images/gene-fusion-cartoon.png){fig-align="center"}

## Cancer fusions

::: {.center}
- **Recurrent** fusions occur in multiple samples
:::

![](./images/erg-tmprss2.png){fig-align="center" width=45%}

![](./images/pcawg-erg-tmprss2-frequency.png){fig-align="center" width=45%}



## Search for recurrent tumor fusions

<br><br>

![](./images/novel-fusion-search.png){fig-align="center" width=60%}

## Gaps in current approaches

::: {.center}

::: {.small}
- RNA-seq focused, **not DNA-seq**
- Assume short, paired-end read sequencing data, not **long read**
- [Single-sample, **not multi-sample**]{.underline}
:::

:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](./images/rna-molecule.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}

<br><br>

![](./images/paired-end-read-sequencing.jpg){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
![](./images/blue-person-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=65%}
:::
::::


## Tumor heterogeneity

::: {.center}
- Low fusion signal
:::

<br>


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="10%"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.center}
![**Single-sample**](./images/blue-person-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=50%}
:::
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](./images/fractional-tumor-cells.png){fig-align="center" width=30%}
:::
::: {.column width="10%"}
:::
::::


## Multiple samples increase sensitivity

::: {.center}
- High aggregate fusion signal
:::

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="25%"}
::: {.center}
![**Single-sample**](./images/blue-person-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=65%}
:::
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](./images/fractional-tumor-cells.png){fig-align="center" width=30%}
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
::: {.center}
![**Multi-sample**](./images/blue-person-cohort.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](./images/fractiona-tumor-cells-grid.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::::

<!-- ## Leveraging corroborating evidence

![](./images/corroborating-dna-and-rna-evidence.png){fig-align="center" width=40%} -->


## Addressing gaps

![](./images/addressing-gaps.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}




## Tumor and control databases

::: {.center}
- 4,647 total human genome samples
:::

![](./images/tissue-type-databases.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}

## Fundamental units of fusion evidence

<br><br>

![](./images/fundamental-units-of-genefusion-evidence-w-cohort.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}

## Fusion equivalence

::: {.center}

- Equivalence is required for recurrency detection

:::

<br><br>

![](./images/erg-tmprss2-breakpoints.png){fig-align="center" width=35%}


## Fusion equivalence

![](./images/erg-tmprss2-breakpoints.png){fig-align="center" width=35%}

![](../results/2026_03-func_equiv_exp/breakpoint_scatter_hist.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}

## Fusion equivalence

![](./images/erg-tmprss2-breakpoints.png){fig-align="center" width=35%}

![](./images/breakpoint-scatter-w-box.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}

## Fusion equivalence

![](./images/erg-tmprss2-breakpoints.png){fig-align="center" width=35%}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="45%"}
![](./images/breakpoint-scatter-w-box.png){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::
::: {.column width="55%"}
![](../results/2026_03-func_equiv_exp/func_equiv_samples.png){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::
::::







## Fusion prioritization

<!-- - **Polymerization** score ranks fusions given multimodal data -->

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}

![](./images/fusion-search-space.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::: {.column width="66%"}
![](./images/score-cartoon.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}
:::
::::

## Prostate ERG-TMPRSS2 evaluation

<!-- ::: {.small}
- Recurrent tumor fusion score is higher than random gene pairs
- PCAWG prostate fusion call (not recurrent) scores are right-skewed
::: -->

![](../results/2025_11-score/prostate/null_v_pcawg_hist.log.no_pcawg.png){fig-align="center"}




## Recurrent tissue classification

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
<br>
![](./images/tissue-specific-labels.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="60%"}
:::{.center}
![**PCAWG recurrent fusions**](./images/pcawg-recurrent-fusion-heatmap-legened-erg-tmprss2-box.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
:::
::::

## Tissue specificity score evaluation


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="60%"}
<br>
![](./images/score-threshold-tissue-specific-labels.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
<br>
![](../results/2026_04-roc_tumor_and_normal_recurrent_fusions/roc_data-neg_tissue_agnostic/roc-best.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::::

## Novel kidney fusion discovery


![](../results/2025_11-score/kidney/null_v_pcawg_hist.log.no_pcawg.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}

![](..//results/2026_04-kidney_shpk_itgae_viz/plot.png){fig-align="center" width=50%}

<!-- :::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2025_11-score/kidney/null_v_pcawg_hist.log.png){width=80% fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](..//results/2026_04-kidney_shpk_itgae_viz/plot.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::: -->

## Discovery potential

![](./images/discovery-potential.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}

## Filtering with control cohort

::: {.center}
- 59,117 benchmark cancer cell line fusions
:::


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/haas-publication.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/haas-onekg-depth-no-title.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}
:::
::::



<!-- :::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](../results/2025_09-haas_onekg/haas_onekg_readcount_histogram.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
![](../results/2025_09-haas_onekg/haas_onekg_readcount_histogram_filt.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
:::: -->

## Acknowledgements

::: {.small}
::: {.center}
- Ryan Layer, Ph.D. 
- Murad Chowdhury
- Ben Braun
- Simon Walker
- Anton Avramov, Ph.D.
- Erik Johnson, Ph.D.
- Funding: NIH 5R01HG011774-04

![](./images/cu-boulder-logo.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}

Department of Computer Science

BioFrontiers Institute

**jacob.krol@colorado.edu**
:::
:::


## Appendix


## Data

::: {.small}
- Tumor and control (WGS & RNA-seq): 2,143 Pan-cancer Analysis of Whole Genomes (PCAWG) samples
- Control (WGS): 2,504 samples from 1000G project
- Total 4,647 human genome samples
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_04-pcawg_sample_counts/sample_counts_tissue.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/1000-genomes.jpg){fig-align="center" width=70%}
:::
::::

:::aside
Variability in tissue, sequencing technology, and disease state across samples
:::

## Read-level resolution

- Read-level fusion evidence is stored for each sample

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](./images/database-solid.png){fig-align="center" width=55%}
![](./images/stix-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=55%}
:::
::: {.column width="66%"}

<br>

![](./images/fundamental-units-of-genefusion-evidence.png){fig-align="center" width=100%}
:::
::::

## Fundamental units of fusion evidence

![](./images/fundamental-units-of-genefusion-evidence-w-cohort.png)

$$
\text{score} = {\color{skyblue}{\text{#reads}}} + {\color{skyblue}{\text{#samples}}} - \text{#reads} - \text{#samples}
$$


## Normalization



::: {.small}
- Normalization accounts for population size and sequencing parameters (coverage)
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/sample-normalization.png){fig-align="center" width=60%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_01-toy-score-plots/tumor_read_score.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::::


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.very-small}
$$
\text{score}_{\text{sample}} \in [0,1] = \frac{\text{#samples}}{\text{Population size}} 
$$
:::
:::

::: {.column width="40%"}
::: {.very-small}
$$
\text{score}_{\text{reads}} \in [0,1] =
\begin{cases}
  \frac{\mathbb{E}[\text{max reads]} - |\text{#reads} - \mathbb{E}[\text{max reads}]|}{\mathbb{E}[\text{max reads}]} & \text{if #reads} \leq 2\cdot \mathbb{E}[\text{max reads}] \\[6pt]
  0 & \text{else}
\end{cases} 
$$
:::
:::

::: {.column width="30%"}

:::
::::

## Score sign interpretation

score $\in \mathbb{R}_{++} \rightarrow$ [tumor]{style="color: skyblue;"} evidence $>$ normal evidence

$$
\text{score} = \frac{1}{2} ({\color{skyblue}{\text{score}_{\text{reads}}}} + {\color{skyblue}{\text{score}_{\text{samples}}}} - \text{score}_{\text{reads}} - \text{score}_{\text{samples}})
$$


$\frac{1}{2}$ scales score to [-1,1] range

Optional mixing parameters: $W_{normal} \in [0,1]$ and $W_{tumor} = 1 - W_{normal}$




<!-- ## Graveyard -->


<!-- ## Problem statement

::: {.small}
- How discover novel recurrent fusions driving human cancer?
- Can population recurrency be used to amplify low fusion signal overlooked by single-sample fusion callers?
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_03-func_equiv_exp/breakpoint_scatter_hist.png){width=70% fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_03-func_equiv_exp/func_equiv_samples.png){width=90% fig-align="center"}
:::
::::
<!-- add star at mean breakpoint -->

<!-- :::{.small}
"Functionally equivalent" variants are necessary to identify recurrency
::: -->


<!-- ## Outline

- Genefusion structural variants (SV)s
- Human genome population databases
- Polymerization: population-scale discovery of tumor driving genefusions
- Evaluation and novel fusion discovery using Polymerization -->

<!-- talk about fusion -->

<!-- :::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](./images/genefusion-diagram.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](./images/stix-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=60%}
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](../results/2025_11-score/prostate/null_v_pcawg_hist.png){fig-align="center" width=85%}
:::
::: {.column width="25%"}
![](../results/2026_04-kidney_shpk_itgae_viz/plot.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
:::: -->

<!-- ::: {.column width="70%"}
![](./images/sv-del-diagram.png){fig-align="center"}
::: -->

<!-- Population databases for *discovery* of SV-disease-association is underexplored -->


<!-- ## Adjacent work

::: {.small}
- Large control cohorts are used for *filtering* structural variants (SV)s
:::

![](./images/filtering-w-pop-sv-databases.png)

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="35%"}
:::
::: {.column width="35%"}
![](./images/gnomad-logo.png){fig-align="center" width=35%}
![](./images/1000-genomes.jpg){fig-align="center" width=35%}
:::
::: {.column width="30%"}
:::
::::

::: aside
Fusions are a special case of SVs
::: -->


<!-- ## Genomic interval databases

- Storage and retrieval of fusions across populations is enabled by genomic interval databases (STIX & GIGGLE)


:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](./images/database-solid.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](./images/stix-cropped.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
![](./images/giggle.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::::

:::aside
STIX & GIGGLE developed by Layer Lab
:::







  
## Current approach

::: {.small}
- Here, population databases are used for *discovery* of tumor-driving fusions
:::

![](./images/discovery-w-pop-sv-databases.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}


:::aside
Need a large cancer cohort to find recurrent fusions
::: -->


<!-- ## Feasibility enabled by databases

::: {.very-small}
- Competing databases are usually summary statistics
- GIGGLE and STIX retain sample-wise read-level evidence
:::

![](../results/2026_04-pcawg_sample_counts/sample_counts_tissue.png){fig-align="center" width=30%}

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](./images/database-solid.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}
:::
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](./images/stix-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
![](./images/giggle.png){fig-align="center" width=40%}
:::
:::: -->


<!-- ## Notes

- Text -> Header
- Ask for extension
- omit equivalent experiment
- slide with gap
- transition from fusion background to population databases
  - stix plus DB
- Use baby fusion photo and recurrency
  - order: fusion -> recurrency importance -> population databases (cohort + database icon)
- adjust text on previous work slide
- replace sample count bar with PCAWG icon
- Crime scene analogy after data
  - after, pose question of how to do it
- erg-tmprss2 evaluation:
  - "How is it doing?"
  - Point out ET2 is well-known fusions
- roc bigger
- remove the pcawg heatmap and just talk about it

** in general, text -> header**
- center format other text -->

<!-- ## Fusion prioritization


- How prioritize most likely tumor-driving fusions given the multimodal evidence ($\mathbf{x}$)?

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_04-pcawg_sample_counts/sample_counts_tissue.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
$$
f(\mathbf{x}) = \text{?}
$$
:::
:::: -->

<!-- ## Conclusions

::: {.small}
- Developed a fusion ranking metric for population-scale data
- Evaluation
  - Separation of recurrent tumor and normal fusions
  - Tissue specificity
- Potential novel fusion discovery: SHPK--ITGAE in kidney cancer
  - Not reported in primary PCAWG publication
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](../results/2026_04-roc_tumor_and_normal_recurrent_fusions/roc_data-neg_normal/roc-best.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}
::: 
::: {.column width="33%"}
![](../results/2026_04-roc_tumor_and_normal_recurrent_fusions/roc_data-neg_tissue_agnostic/roc-best.png){fig-align="center" width=70%}
:::
::: {.column width="34%"}
![](../results/2026_04-kidney_shpk_itgae_viz/plot.png){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::
::::


## Next steps

::: {.small}
- Potential collaboration with Anschutz Children's hospital
- Further visual verification 
  - Multiple samples
- Add probabilistic score interpretation
:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_04-kidney_shpk_itgae_viz/plot.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2025_11-score/kidney/null_v_pcawg_hist.log.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
:::: -->

<!-- ## Recurrent normal vs. tumor classification {.small}

<!-- - Score threshold separated recurrent tumor fusions from recurrent normal fusions -->

<!-- <br><br>

:::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="60%"}
![](./images/score-threshold.png)
:::
::: {.column width="40%"}
![](../results/2026_04-roc_tumor_and_normal_recurrent_fusions/roc_data-neg_normal/roc-best.png){fig-align="center"}
:::
::::

## Low scoring recurrent tumor fusions

<br>

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/roc-tumor-v-normal-red-box.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](../results/2026_04-s2f_pcawg_recurrent/scatter-tumor_reads_score.png){fig-align="center" width=90%}
:::
::: -->


<!-- :::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/paper-pcawg-recurrent-fusion.png){fig-align="center" width=65%}
:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/normal-fusion-paper.png){fig-align="center" width=65%}
:::
:::: --> 

<!-- ## Fusions in human cancer

::: {.center}

::: {.small}
:::

:::

:::: {.columns}
::: {.column width="50%"}

::: {.center}
![PCAWG recurrent fusions](./images/pcawg-recurrent-fusion-heatmap-legened-erg-tmprss2-box.png){fig-align="center"}
:::

:::
::: {.column width="50%"}
![](./images/erg-tmprss2.png){fig-align="center"}
![](./images/pcawg-logo-cropped.png){fig-align="center" width=80%}
:::
:::: -->
